headlines.title: Schoolgirl Bathing in Lake Togo Rescued From Drowning
headlines.subtitle: Le Roy Eldridge, Rescuer, Twice Has Saved Life at Same Place.
headlines.fullText:
Miss Mary Lewis, one of the young girl students of All Saints Academy, Rosen Heights, was saved from drown- ing in Lake Togo at White City Mon- day afternoon by Le or Blanc 2:03 Azle avenue, an emplose orme Fort Worth Light and Power Com-
Miss Lewis was a member of a bath- Ing party from the academy. This party girls are said to know how to swim. but the sisters had forbidden them to go into deep water.
Miss Lewis was swimming around and when she went down she found she could not touch bottom. She sank into water over her head. As she came up fright seized her and she began to Eldridge, his wife and several others, were having a pienie on the other side a manand the fake, W. B. Baxter, foreman of the lard re- finery at Armour him. Eldridge jumped into the water and grabbed hold of the drowning girl.
She is said to have locked his arms is her struggle, and the timely arrival of Baxter saved both. Eldridge saved Johnny Myers, a boy, from drowning in the same lake a year ago.

headlines.title: President Wilson Kicks in With $50 for Bale of Cotton
headlines.fullText: WASHINGTON, Sept. 8 .- President Wilson today joined the "Buy-a-bale- of-cotton" club by ordering from the head of the organization in deorell 444 worth of cotton. Senator Hoke Smith told him that people throughout the South were joining the movement to take up the cotton crop left over by Senator a senator Smith also told the Presi- would be ent halt and that wheat and other products would be substituted.

headlines.title: LAKE WORTH DAM WILL BE EXTENDED
headlines.subtitle: Three- Foot Additional Rise Would Have Sent Water Around End of Embankment.
headlines.fullText:

headlines.title: Players' Company Scores Hit; Majestic Music Good
headlines.subtitle: Fort Worth Girl Proves Successful in Ingenue Role-Gwent Singers Offer Real Melody.
headlines.fullText:
A deservedly warm welcome greeted fas an actor in the one short scene In which he appeared the Players Company Monday night for the opening of the winter stock sea- son at the Savoy theater, as Manager Sport North has gathered together one of the most capable and best balanced stock companies ever seen in the Southwest.
"The Spendthrift" is this week's bill, and gives especial opportunities to Miss Genevieve Russell, leading woman of the company, who is new to theater- goers here, but is well and favorably known as a stock star all through the Middle West. As the selfish, frivolous wife who has driven her husband to bankruptcy by her extravagance, Miss Russell made quite a hit. A touch of comedy added to the part by her re- fusal to take his business entangle menta seriously and the heavy emigr Beheben suspects her of unfaith- en fulness, afford an opportunity to show her great versatility.
The early acts also gave her an op- portunity to wear some particularly handsome gowns. Evelyn Kincaid, a Fort Worth girl, made good in an Ingenue more than mace euro shown by the Audience gave evidence of her popu- larity here. Elaborate floral tributes were presented to both Miss Russell and Miss Kincaid.
Frederick Hand, the leading man of the company, carried off a father dite thenit part, weil showing men forer but no mit or mara Miss Pelain Gates played ,opposite bliss miseje equally well. Sport North, whose reputation as a character actor is well established, had a very congenial Hand. Miss Gertrude Stone also made much of her opportunities in the char- Beter part of Atint Gretchen. Frederick Clarke, the stage director, deserver much credit for the smoothness of the production, and also showed his ability
AT THE MAJESTIC.
It is well that the Royal Gwent Welsh Male Singers top this week's Majestic bill. It is a very ordinary entertainment otitside of these young men, although every act on the pro- gram le good in widely scattered spots. The Welsh singers made themselves firm in Fort Worth last season and rector George F. Davies is a master, 'george and the fifteen young men of the cho rus have perfect harmony and rich vol- ume of volee. Marie Stoddard is the next best bet. tnasmuch as she must be compared with the headliner. Her "vodevilje types" are good, but her "Sis Hopkins" work needs planing.
Foster Ball, assisted by Fred Stan- ton, furnishes an interesting bit of the Days of "61." but half of the sketch could be dropped without a loss of applause.
Lettle Williams & Co. manage to get by with a line of Pell street slang and maybe the otherbe blamed for the mums par melodrama of the set. The curtain comes down on an ending so weak that it causes wonderment for enduring so long.
Chinko is a deft young juggler and uses a novel set of properties. Per- haps the audience she had dropped day night because he men megling things braten Han he- Tuer best was finished. The stage was being set for something that looked interesting, but there was nothing for febuitmuur Chinko to do but gult with his work and friend of Richard Ward, played by Newkirk and the two Evans sisters offer a sequence of ragtime songs, 80- elety dances and display of costumery. reluctantly accepted by an audience med to better acts of the kind. Minnie Kaufmann in a bicycle act served as an opener for the show.

headlines.title: Lane Goes to Hearing.
headlines.fullText: AUSTIN, Sept. 8 .-- W. P. Lane leaves tonight for Houston to attend his con- Her High comes up for bearing Rent 10 in the Sixty-first district court.

headlines.title: 2 BLAMED FOR GERMAN BREAK IN DIPLOMACY
headlines.subtitle: Reported in Rome Chancellor and Foreign Minister in Berlin Resign.
headlines.fullText:
ROME, Sept. 8 .- According to a dis- patch from Berlin to the Messagarro it is said that in the controversy which has arisen between the German em- peror and Chancellor von Bethmann- Hollweg and Minister of Foreign Af- fairs von Jagow, the two ministers are being regarded as responsible for the when to the coalition of Euro- sean states against Germany.
It is reported, accordthe to the pub- lished dispatch, that their resignations have been tendered.

headlines.title: 70,000 Austrians Concentrated.
headlines.fullText: LONDON, Sept. has concentrated 70,000 men at Reben- leo, Dalmatia, in preparation for any International developments.

headlines.title: Loses $150 Diamond.
headlines.fullText: Charles F. Hayne, living at the Melba Hotel, has reported to detectives the loss of a diamond stud valued at $150, He sent it to the laundry in his shirt he said. Detectives made an Investi- gation, but were unable to get any trace of It.

headlines.title: WILSON ASKS PRAYERS N FOR PEACE IN EUROPE.
headlines.fullText: WASHINGTON, Sept. - 8. President Wilson today signed people of the J'aited States to Pray for peace in Fuente, ana cets aside Sunday, Oct. 4, at the date.
